这家位于英国爱沙尼亚的公司正在使用从金融科技和远程医疗领域借鉴的技术，包括要求用户在进行家庭测试时进行拍摄，以大胆地尝试解决远程测试的依从性和信任问题。
 最初以英国的私人和企业为目标，随后是其他市场，可以在线订购测试，并可以在第二天进行远程测试，并保证在第二天获得认证的结果进行PCR测试，并在90分钟之内进行抗原测试。
  更广泛地说，Certific应用程序和用户历程旨在提高对远程测试的信任，并确保自我执行的测试达到与临床环境相同的标准。
 “大流行时，我们开始进行第一次手指点刺试验，看看您是否有抗体，然后想，'嘿，有没有办法利用这些抗体来帮助整个世界，'” Hinrikus在采访中告诉我本周初接受采访。 “但是，结果证明，这类抗体测试以及最终免疫是一个非常不清楚的概念。因此，我们将其搁置，但我们一直在思考可以做些更好的事情以使测试更值得信赖和更容易。”
 然后在去年下半年，他和Certific的其他共同创始人-医师Jack Kreindler博士兼首席执行官Liis Narusk –意识到有些事情“我们可以并且应该做些事情，以提出一种更加民主化的医学测试方法，并加以应用流行”。
 Hinrikus并没有完全说出来，但是作为TransferWise的创始人和多产的天使投资人，包括支持爱沙尼亚验证平台Veriff的人，毫无疑问，Certific一定程度上受到了在金融技术领域中倍受关注的身份验证技术（例如视频自拍）的启发过去曾在数字银行吸引新客户。此外，医学总监克林德勒（Kreindler）在近战运动中具有反兴奋剂方面的经验。
  结合更传统的身份检查，Certific应用程序会要求您在参加考试时进行拍摄。记录和测试结果将安全地上传到Certific，并由合格的医生检查，以确保您正确遵守制造商的说明。然后将包含测试结果的医学证明发送回应用程序。 PCR检测的价格为64英镑，很快上市的快速抗原检测将以12包的价格出售，价格为249英镑（单抗原检测的价格为20.75英镑）。 
但是欺骗测试并假冒结果有多容易呢？ Certific首席执行官Narusk说：“这绝对是我们非常非常专注于解决问题的一件事。” “我们在所有反欺诈和反欺诈领域的经验意味着我们已经走了很远，以确保您不会真正篡改该过程。因此，当您录制视频时，以及录制完视频之后，我们的测试验证人员都会对其进行检查，以确保您没有将测试移离屏幕。”
 另外，Certific会确保您使用的测试实际上是您订购的测试，并且包含相同的唯一ID，并且您应该被认为是进行测试的人。
 这本身并不完全是欺诈的证明，Hinrikus澄清说Certific最初将重点放在确保测试在医学上正确进行。他说，更高级别的价格将在稍后阶段提供增强的视频验证，例如现场操作员充当见证人。
 这对于现场活动或旅行等企业可能特别有用，因为这可能会诱使个人作弊，并且可能需要运营商向保险公司或政府机构证明他们的COVID-19安全。
 Certificate的医学总监Kreindler将其与英国当局目前允许进行冠状病毒家庭测试的关键工人进行了对比，而他们没有进行任何额外的核实，但假冒结果的可能性不大。
 “如果您考虑一下，那些公务员如果测试积极就不会处于很大的劣势，因为他们仍然能得到报酬。因此，作弊的动机更少。挑战来自在环境中进行护理测试的地方，在这种环境中，[对阳性测试]确实存在一些动机或很大的不利条件。”
 克林德勒还说，这不仅关乎个人，而且Certific还与爱沙尼亚，北美和英国的学者合作，开发了一种计算风险模型，用于大规模事件等“超级撒布机”环境的大规模测试。人们不仅可以在参加会议之前在家中进行测试，而且可以不断学习并考虑“民主的分散化测试”以及对疫苗接种和免疫的理解的风险模型可以使进一步的缓解措施得以实施。确保该病毒没有净传播回社区。他说：“这是我们未来思考的核心。” “这不只是证明测试，还涉及证明人群”。 
进一步缩小-超越当前的冠状病毒大流行-已将Certific建立为完全不可知的。 结合速度，便利性，坚持和信任，该公司的目标是成为现有和将来的家庭测试可以运行的轨道（我的话，不是他们的话）。 将来，这可能会涵盖性传播疾病（SDI）的检测到运动中的反兴奋剂检测。 而且，当然，随着新型COVID-19测试的到来。
note_en:
The British-Estonian company is using techniques borrowed from the worlds of fintech and telemedicine, including asking users to film themselves while taking the at-home test, in a bold attempt to solve remote testing’s adherence and trust problem.
 Initially targeting private individuals and businesses in the U.K., with other markets to follow, tests can be ordered online and are carried out remotely with the promise of a certified result the following day for PCR tests and in under 90 minutes for antigen tests.
  More broadly, the Certific app and user journey is designed to increase trust in remote testing and ensure that self-performed tests reach the same standard as those carried out in a clinical setting.
 “When the pandemic hit, we started toying around with the first finger prick tests to see if you have antibodies, and thinking, ‘hey, is there a way to make use of these to help the world along,’ ” Hinrikus told me during an interview earlier this week. “[But] then it turned out that these kind of antibody tests, and the end immunity, was a very unclear concept. And so we kind of put it on hold, but we kept on thinking about what better things we can do to make testing more trustworthy and easier”.
 Then late last year he and Certific’s other co-founders — physician Dr. Jack Kreindler and CEO Liis Narusk — realised that there were things “that we can and should be doing to come up with a more democratised way of medical testing, and apply this to the pandemic”.
 Hinrikus doesn’t quite say it, but as the founder of TransferWise and a prolific angel investor, including backing Estonian verification platform Veriff, there’s little doubt that Certific is partly inspired by the authentication techniques that have gained prominence in fintech, such as video selfies used to onboard new customers at digital banks. In addition, medical director Kreindler has experience with anti-doping in close-combat sports.
  Coupled with more traditional identity checks, the Certific app asks you to film yourself while you take the test. The recording and test result is securely uploaded to Certific and checked by a qualified physician to ensure you have adhered to the manufacturer’s instructions properly. A medical certification containing the test result is then delivered back to the app. PCR tests cost £64, and the soon to be available rapid antigen tests will be sold in 12-packs for £249 (making the price of a single antigen test £20.75).
 But how easy would it be to cheat the test and therefore fake a result? “That’s one thing we definitely are very, very focused on solving,” says Certific CEO Narusk. “Our experience in all the anti-fraud and anti-cheat areas means that we went far and beyond to make sure that you can’t actually tamper with the process. So when you record the video, and after you have recorded the video, it is checked by our test verification officers who make sure that you haven’t moved the tests away from the screen”.
 In addition, Certific ensures that the test you have used is actually the test that you ordered and contains the same unique ID, and that you are the person who was supposed to do the test.
 That in itself isn’t entirely fraud proof, and Hinrikus clarifies that Certific is initially focusing on ensuring that a test is carried out medically correctly. He says that a higher-priced tier will be offered at a later stage with enhanced video verification, such as a live operator acting as a witness.
 This could be particularly useful for businesses, such as live events or travel, where there could be incentives for individuals to cheat and where operators may be required to prove to insurance companies or government authorities that they are COVID-19 safe.
 Kreindler, Certific’s medical director, contrasts this with key workers that are currently permitted by U.K. authorities to carry out coronavirus home-testing without any additional verification, but who aren’t nearly as likely to want to fake a result.
 “If you think about it, those public servants are not at a great disadvantage if they test positive, because they still get paid. So there’s less of an incentive to cheat. And the challenge comes where you are doing point of care testing in an environment where there actually is some incentive or a big disadvantage [to testing positive]”.
 Kreindler also says it’s not just about individuals and that Certific has worked with academics in Estonia, North America and in the U.K. to develop a computational risk model for mass testing for “super spreader” environments, such as large events. People will not only be able to take a test at home before attending, but a risk model that continually learns and takes into account “democratised decentralised testing” and an understanding of vaccination and immunity, could enable further mitigations to be put in place to make sure there’s no net spread of the virus back into the community. “That’s very core to our thinking going forward,” he says. “It’s not just about certifying testing, it’s also about certifying crowds”.
 Zooming out even further — and beyond the current coronavirus pandemic — Certific has been built to be entirely test agnostic. Combining speed, convenience, adherence and trust, the company aims to be the rails on which existing and future home tests can run (my words, not theirs). In the future, this could span testing for sexually transmitted diseases (SDIs) to anti-doping tests in sports. And, of course, new types of COVID-19 tests as they come on stream.
title: Certific是一家由TransferWise创始人创立的健康科技初创公司，旨在成为认证家庭测试的关键
title_en: Certific, a health tech startup from the founder of TransferWise, aims to be the rails for certified home testing

note:
一家初创公司正在开发针对这一部分人群的金融产品，目的是帮助他们建立信贷，节省资金，获取资金并为未来做计划。
 该初创公司SeedFi周三宣布已筹集了5,000万美元的债务，并在由Andreessen Horowitz（也称为a16z）领导的股权融资中筹集了1,500万美元。这家风险投资公司在2019年3月成立时，还领导了SeedFi的400万美元种子资金。
   SeedFi成立的前提是许多美国人在经济上难以取得成功。它的创始团队曾在初创公司和大型银行（如摩根大通（JPMorgan Chase）和资本一号（Capital One））中工作，其前提是许多传统金融机构并非旨在帮助陷入财务困境的美国人取得成功。
 SeedFi联合创始人兼首席执行官Jim McGinley说：“我们已经亲眼目睹了该系统是如何设计的，以帮助贫困的美国人解决问题。” “我们的平均客户每年可赚取50,000美元，但他们每年需支付460美元的透支费，发薪日贷款公司会向他们收取400％或更高的年利率。他们勉强能负担开支，任何失误都可能使他们退缩数年。”
  他告诉TechCrunch：“我了解到他们所面临的财务困难以及对更好产品的需求，以帮助他们进一步发展。”
 联合创始人埃里克·伯顿（Eric Burton）说，他之所以能够建立关系，是因为他在得克萨斯州中部长大，是“超级贫困家庭”的一员。
 他回忆说：“我经历了低收入的种种挣扎和获得高价信贷以度过日常的必要性。” “我个人长期陷入债务循环。” 
实际上，他从Capital One获得的一份工作提议被暂时撤销，因为该公司表示他“信誉欠佳”，这实际上是他18岁时未付的医疗费用的结果。
 他说：“我对他们一无所知，但在用我的签约奖金还清债务后能够找到工作。” “因此，我可以理解特定的起点是如何使其很难实现的。”
 SeedFi的目标是解决问题的根源。它于2019年以私人Beta版推出，甚至在COVID-19大流行期间，也帮助其最初的客户节省了超过500,000美元的资金。
 现在，它将通过两种产品向公众推出。一种是建立信用的产品，旨在“建立重要的长期储蓄习惯”。客户从每张薪水中只能节省10美元，这已报告给信贷机构以建立其信用记录，然后能够在六个月内节省500美元。
 据该公司称，经过六个月的按时付款后，没有信用记录的SeedFi客户能够建立600的信用评分，而具有现有信用评分和少于三个信用帐户的客户则将其信用评分提高了45分。
 使消费者能够超越传统方法建立信用记录的概念变得越来越普遍。就在上周，我们写了有关Tomo Credit的文章，它为客户提供借记卡，以便他们可以根据现金流量建立信用。
  SeedFi的另一项产品，借贷“成长计划”旨在使分期付款或发薪日贷款的价格更便宜。它为消费者提供了“立即获得”资金的渠道，同时还帮助他们建立了储蓄和信贷渠道。 
加入种子融资董事会的Andreessen Horowitz普通合伙人Angela Strange认为，“新金融服务进入者将有更好的产品体验，承销和技术，为历史上服务欠缺的人群提供巨大的商机。”
 在博客文章中，她分享了SeedFi工作原理的示例。该公司评估风险并向传统上难以承保的客户提供信贷。它决定了放贷的数量，以及现在作为储蓄还是储蓄的美元所占比例。
 “例如，典型的SeedFi计划的结构可能是现在为500美元，在储蓄帐户中保留500美元。借款人会随着时间的流逝还清$ 1,000，在计划结束时，他或她的储蓄帐户中有$ 500。借款人不仅做出了较低的利率支付，而且在做出借款决定后，他或她的财务状况也更好。”
 展望未来，SeedFi计划利用其新资金建立其产品套件并扩大其客户群。
 麦金利说：“我们将能够更有效地为不断增长的贷款组合提供资金，并为更多的客户提供服务。”
note_en:
One startup is developing financial products targeted toward this segment of the population, with the goal of helping them build credit, save money, access funds and plan for the future.
 That startup,   SeedFi , announced Wednesday it has raised $50 million in debt and $15 million in an equity funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz, also known as a16z. The VC firm also led SeedFi’s $4 million seed funding when it was founded in March of 2019.
   SeedFi was founded on the premise that it is difficult for many Americans to get ahead financially. Its founding team has worked at both startups and big banks, such as JPMorgan Chase and Capital One, and operates under the premise that many legacy financial institutions are simply not designed to help Americans who are struggling financially to get ahead.
 “We’ve seen firsthand how the system has been designed for underprivileged Americans to fail,” said Jim McGinley, co-founder and CEO of SeedFi. “Our average customer earns $50,000 a year, yet they pay $460 a year in overdraft fees and payday loan companies charge them APRs of 400% or more. They barely make enough to cover their expenses and any misstep can set them back for years.”
  “There I got insights to the financial difficulties they had and the need for better products to help them get a step up,” he told TechCrunch.
 Co-founder Eric Burton said he can relate because he grew up in Central Texas as part of “a super poor family.”
 “I experienced all the struggles of being low income and the necessity of taking on high-priced credit to get through day to day,” he recalled. “I personally was trapped in a debt cycle for a long time.”
 In fact, a job offer he got from Capital One was temporarily rescinded because the company said he had “bad credit,” which turned out to be a result of unpaid medical bills he’d incurred at the age of 18.
 “I didn’t know about them, but was able to get the job after using my signing bonus to pay off that debt,” he said. “So I can understand how a certain starting point makes it very hard to progress.”
 SeedFi’s goal is to tackle the root of the problem. It launched in private beta in 2019, and helped its initial customers build more than $500,000 in savings — even during the COVID-19 pandemic.
 Now, it’s launching  to the public with two offerings. One is a credit building product that is designed to “create important long-term savings habits.” Customers save as little as $10 from every paycheck, which is reported to the credit bureaus to build their credit history, and are then able to generate $500 in savings in six months’ time.
 After six months of on-time payments, SeedFi customers with no credit history were able to establish a credit score of 600, while customers with existing credit scores and less than three credit accounts boosted their scores by 45 points, according to the company.
 The concept of enabling consumers to build credit history beyond traditional methods is becoming increasingly more common. Just last week, we wrote about  Tomo Credit, which provides customers with a debit card so they can build credit based on their cash flow.
  SeedFi’s other offering, the Borrow & Grow Plan, is designed to be a more affordable alternative to installment or payday loans. It provides consumers with “immediate access” to funds while also helping them build savings and credit.
 Andreessen Horowitz general partner   Angela Strange  , who has joined SeedFi’s board with the financing, believes there’s “a massive business opportunity for new financial services entrants to reach historically underserved populations through better product experiences, underwriting and technology.”
 In a blog post, she shares an example of how SeedFi works. The company evaluates risk and extends credit to a customer that might be traditionally hard to underwrite. It determines how much to lend, as well as the proportion of dollars to give as money now versus savings.
 “For instance, a typical SeedFi plan might be structured as $500 right now and $500 reserved in a savings account. The borrower pays off $1,000 over time, and at the end of the plan, he or she has $500 in a savings account. Not only has the borrower paid a lower interest rate, he or she is in a   better  financial position after making the decision to borrow money,” Strange writes.
 Looking ahead, SeedFi plans to use its new capital to build out its product suite and grow its customer base.
 “We will be able to more efficiently fund our growing loan portfolio and serve more customers,” McGinley said.
title: SeedFi融资6500万美元，以帮助陷入财务困境的美国人取得成功
title_en: SeedFi closes on $65M to help financially struggling Americans get ahead

note:
在所谓的BelleÉpoque年代的20世纪初，艺术和科学在巴黎蓬勃发展。这是诺贝尔物理学奖得主玛丽·居里（Marie Curie）和她的丈夫皮埃尔（Pierre）在放射性方面取得突破性发现的时候，发现了两个新元素。同时，一个名叫LoïeFuller的现代舞者和舞台灯光的先驱，在巴黎风靡一时，梦想着将镭融入她的舞台表演中。科学作家兼传播者利兹·海内克（Liz Heinecke）在这本富有远见的新传记《光辉：科学家，舞蹈家和光明铸就的友谊》中汇集了这两位有远见的女性的生活。
 玛丽·居里（Marie Curie）的生活细节非常有据可查，并且众所周知。她离开家乡波兰，十四岁时移居巴黎，攻读科学学位，在学习和进行研究时生活在极度贫困中。她遇到了一位名叫皮埃尔·居里（Pierre Curie）的化学家，两人开始合作，最终坠入爱河并于1895年结婚。居里夫妇结婚了六个月，当时威廉·伦琴（Wilhelm Roentgen）发现了X射线（1901年获得第一届诺贝尔物理学奖） 。此后不久，亨利·贝克勒尔（Henri Becquerel）发表了他的见解，即铀盐发出的射线会在照相底片上产生雾。贝克勒尔（Becquerel）的铀射线使玛丽深深着迷，以至于她使玛丽成为自己研究的重点。
  与皮埃尔一起，她发现了两种被称为evidence和镭的新元素的证据。这对夫妻与贝克勒尔（Becquerel）共同分享了1903年诺贝尔物理学奖，这是因为他们的工作发展了放射性理论-她是第一位获得如此荣誉的女人。皮埃尔（Pierre）在1906年的一场街头事故中不幸去世后，玛丽（Marie）开发了从沥青藻中分离放射性同位素的新技术，并最终于1910年成功分离了镭。她于1911年因发现而获得第二届诺贝尔奖（这次是化学） lon和镭。她仍然是唯一两次获得诺贝尔奖的女性，也是在两个不同科学领域中唯一获得此殊荣的人。
  另一方面，罗伊·富勒（LoïeFuller）在很大程度上已经沦落为历史的脚注，尽管近年来对她的职业生涯和影响力重新产生了兴趣-特别是在泰勒·斯威夫特（Taylor Swift）2018年声望期间的精选细分领域中巡演以及编舞乔迪·斯珀林（Jody Sperling）的作品。她于1862年生于芝加哥郊区，是一个儿童女演员，后来开始编舞并表演自己的舞蹈，通常在滑稽表演，杂耍表演和马戏团表演场所进行。她开始尝试一条长裙，以不同的方式移动它，以探索它是如何反射光线的。到1891年，她已经弄清楚了如何在表演期间用不同颜色的灯光照亮她的丝绸服装，以创造醒目的视觉效果。她是一位自学成才的化学家，最终获得了使用各种化合物和盐来产生彩色凝胶和发光照明的专利权。
 富勒以她的签名而声名狼藉并获得好评。蛇纹舞（尽管富勒不是舞者的特色，但卢米埃（Lumière）兄弟在1897年的电影中被捕获）。根据舞蹈史学家杰克·安德森（Jack Anderson）的说法，她的“蛇纹舞”服装是由数百码的中国丝绸组成的，她将滚滚的巨浪环绕在她身上，而灯光效果则表明它着火并且形状令人联想起花朵，云彩，鸟类和蝴蝶。＆＃34;
 las，其他舞池较少的舞者很快将她的作品用于自己的表演，这促使沮丧的富勒（Fuller）到欧洲巡回演出，希望对她的艺术获得更多的认可。她在巴黎FoliesBergère的表演取得了巨大的成功，她成为了那里的常客，表演了她的＆＃34; Fire Dance＆＃34;以及“蛇纹舞”。她是当地新艺术运动风格的固定装置，与亨利·德·图卢兹-洛特雷克（Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec），奥古斯特·罗丹（Auguste Rodin），史泰芬·玛尔玛（StéphaneMallarmé）等众多当代艺术家和作家共舞。她还是美国舞蹈演员伊莎多拉·邓肯（Isadora Duncan）的良师益友，并因公开决定与女同性恋伴侣公开生活而受到公众的批评。
    皮埃尔（Pierre）和居里（Marie Curie）参加了在弗利斯贝尔热（FoliesBergère）举行的富勒（Fuller）表演之一，并留下了深刻的印象。钦佩是相互的：富勒被居里夫妇深深吸引。她于1905年写给他们的镭实验，询问用镭制作服装的可能性（她不知道供应量有多有限）。玛丽礼貌地建议不要这样做，尽管她自己喜欢随身携带镭瓶，并且喜欢晚上去实验室，因为发光管看上去像仙女的灯。富勒不为所动，将荧光盐加工成黑色纱布连衣裙，她穿着它来表演＆＃34;镭舞，＆＃34;当她在黑暗的舞台上旋转时，营造出她周围闪烁着星星或幽灵般的灯光的幻觉。 
乍一看，这两个女人似乎并没有太多共同点，但是在《辐射》杂志中，海纳克（Heinecke）在改变各自领域并结成不可能的友谊时，挑逗了他们之间一些有趣的相似之处。 Ars与Heinecke坐下来了解更多信息。
 Ars Technica：居里夫人实际上是个家喻户晓的名字，但LoïeFuller却不是。因此，我很好奇，是什么驱使您将这两个女人聚在一起写成一本书？
 莉兹·海内克（Liz Heinecke）：我正在阅读夏娃·居里（Eve Curie）的母亲的传记，并碰到了LoïeFuller的名字。我上大学时是美术专业。我的女儿是个戏剧孩子，尽管我自己不是舞者，但我喜欢看舞蹈。我用谷歌搜索卢埃·富勒，立刻着迷。最终，我找到了纽约公共表演艺术图书馆数据库，发现他们有一点她的作品可以在线观看。我发现她对放射性很感兴趣，认识了托马斯·爱迪生（Thomas Edison），是奥古斯特·罗丹（Auguste Rodin）的朋友。她认识所有这些有趣的人，而我从未听说过她。她基本上是现代舞台灯光的先驱。当我发现Marie和Loïe彼此之间在五年之内出生和死亡时，我决定将他们两个一起写成书，并使他们的故事以平行的叙述相交。
 Ars Technica：玛丽·居里（Marie Curie）和洛伊·富勒（LoïE·Fuller）至少见过一次面，但是您认为他们之间的纽带是什么？
 Liz Heinecke：好奇心。洛伊是一个非常好奇的人。最终，Loïe可能是与Pierre和Marie保持联系的动力，因为她真的很喜欢科学。她受过良好的教育，但她很聪明，并且知道技术是让她脱颖而出的原因。她之所以去玛丽和皮埃尔的实验室，是因为她对镭和放射性很好奇。我认为玛丽也对Loie的世界感到好奇。我们知道玛丽和皮埃尔很喜欢艺术和戏剧，而洛伊则把他们带到罗丹的工作室看他的作品。
 他们两个都不完美。玛丽一生中都犯了错误，但她是一个更认真，细心的人，而Loïe只是在那里。但是我喜欢这种对比，并且我是这样写的。我的意思是，如果您阅读Loïe关于Marie的文章，她总是觉得自己永远不会对Marie八卦。她总是写道，玛丽非常私密。我想尊重她。＆＃34;只要人们参加她的表演，她就不会在乎人们对她的看法。
  Ars Technica：尽管技术上是非小说类的，但您还是选择以小说的风格来非常多地写作《辐射》。这是为什么？ 
莉兹·海纳克（Liz Heinecke）：我喜欢一个很好的非小说类叙事，尤其是关于科学的叙事。当我开始考虑这个故事时，尤其是Loïe，它是如此的视觉化。我想象过Loïe在舞台上跳舞。我可以想象玛丽和皮埃尔在他们实验室里被发光的镭管包围着。所以我真的很想写它，使它读起来更像小说。我觉得我可以接触到不同的读者，写一本书，读起来更像是一本小说，而不是一本直接的科学叙述或一本传记。
 我在书的开头指出，对话是内部和外部发明的。我根据对两个女人的了解撰写了对话。一年阅读完我能找到的每份文件后，我觉得我几乎了解它们。我知道罗伊（Loïe）喝了黑咖啡和玛丽（Marie）喝了茶，他们在生活的不同部分吃了什么，以及他们担心什么。但是我试图将所有对话都建立在我发现的事实之上。当然，所有场景都基于生活中实际发生的事实事件。例如，即使您读过LoïeFuller，也没有人真正谈论过她在夏至时在艾菲尔铁塔上跳舞的事实。艾菲尔铁塔中涉及的科学太多，我可以写整整本书，大约一个晚上，以及在那里的所有有趣的人。
 Ars Technica：我不知道LoïeFuller会见了Thomas Edison。这是有道理的，因为他参与了最先进的声音和照明以及运动图片，并且她对艺术的科学方面很感兴趣。自从C.P.斯诺（Snow）在1959年创造了这个名词。但是，从根本上讲，科学是文化，反之亦然。 LoïeFuller对此进行了封装。
 Liz Heinecke：是的。她非常擅长将不同的技术融合在一起并创造新的东西。你几乎可以写一个关于Loïe和Edison的平行故事。他们俩都没有受过良好的教育，但是他们都是当今社交媒体上最受欢迎的人的前身。他们俩都非常擅长自我提升。他们俩都非常认真地培养了自己的形象。他们让我感到非常相似。
 当不同学科融合在一起时，我会爱。我觉得今天的社会变得如此分裂，在1900年的巴黎也是如此。科学家大多与科学家们在一起。我以为玛丽居里夫人（Marie Curie）向这位舞蹈演员敞开了自己的世界，这真是太酷了！这确实丰富了他们的生活。当您看到人们的创造力发生巨大爆炸时（当人们离开自己的领域时），我感觉就像那样。
  Ars Technica：写这本书是否激发了您尝试重新夺回BelleÉpoque的一些跨学科精神？
 丽兹·海内克（Liz Heinecke）：没有什么比去图书馆阅读旧文件有趣的了，它试图找到尚未有人发现的新东西。而且，您可以在家中进行许多科学实验。我目前正在为孩子们写一本物理书，并且有一个关于在玻璃上创建薄层的实验室。我想，很酷，让我们尝试在玻璃上放一薄层明胶，然后给它上色，看看是否能制作出可以支撑住光的载玻片，＆＃34;就像罗伊（Loïe）的彩色照明。 
制造居里镜也很容易，这是居里夫妇用来测量放射性的设备的基本概念，它会释放出电荷。 他们使用一种验电器来旋转镜子，使他们能够测量不同材料的放射性。 这就是让玛丽进行放射性的首次真实测量的原因。  我喜欢那个时代的人们如何能够用显微镜非常近距离地观察事物，而第一次使用望远镜却可以很远地观察事物。 他们可以识别出显微镜下的某些物体看起来像天体。 洛伊会拿走她在科学家实验室或显微镜下看到的东西并将其投射在舞台上。 艺术家仍在这样做。 当今，现代科学家们用显微镜和望远镜制作出了精美的艺术品。
note_en:
Both the arts and the sciences flourished in Paris during the years of the so-called Belle Époque at the dawn of the 20th century. This was when Nobel Prize-winning physicist  Marie Curie and her husband, Pierre, made their breakthrough discoveries in radioactivity, discovering two new elements. At the same time, a modern dancer and pioneer in theatrical lighting named  Loïe Fuller, who was all the rage in Paris, dreamed of incorporating radium into her stage act. Science writer and communicator Liz Heinecke brings the live of these two visionary women together in an illuminating new biography,  Radiant: The Scientist, the Dancer, and a Friendship Forged in Light.
 The details of  Marie Curie's life are very well-documented and well-known. She left her native Poland and moved to Paris at 14 to pursue a degree in science, living in abject poverty while studying and conducting research. She met a chemist named Pierre Curie, and they began collaborating, eventually falling in love and getting married in 1895. The Curies had been married for six months when  Wilhelm Roentgen discovered X-rays (winning the very first Nobel Prize in physics in 1901). Soon after,  Henri Becquerel published his insight that uranium salts emitted rays that would fog a photographic plate in early 1896. Becquerel's uranium rays so fascinated Marie that she made them the focus of her own research.
  With Pierre, she uncovered evidence of two new elements they dubbed polonium and radium. The couple shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Becquerel for their work developing a theory of radioactivity—she was the first woman to be so honored. After Pierre's tragic death in a 1906 street accident, Marie developed new techniques for isolating radioactive isotopes from pitchblende and eventually succeeded in isolating radium in 1910. She won a second Nobel Prize (this time in chemistry) in 1911 for the discovery of polonium and radium. She remains the only woman to win the Nobel Prize twice and the only person to do so in two different scientific fields.
  Loïe Fuller, on the other hand, has been largely relegated to the footnotes of history, although there has been a resurgence of interest in her career and influence in recent years—most notably in a featured segment during Taylor Swift's 2018 Reputation Tour, and the work of choreographer  Jody Sperling. Born in the Chicago suburbs in 1862, she was a child actress who later began choreographing and performing her own dances, usually in burlesque, vaudeville, and circus venues. She started experimenting with a long skirt, moving it in different ways to explore how it reflected light, and by 1891, she had figured out how to illuminate her silk costumes during performances with lights of different colors to create striking visual effects. She was something of a self-taught chemist, eventually patenting the use of various chemical compounds and salts to create color gel and luminescent lighting.
 Fuller achieved notoriety and rave reviews with her signature " Serpentine Dance" ( captured on film by the  Lumière brothers in 1897, although Fuller was not the dancer featured). According to dance historian Jack Anderson, "The costume for her  Serpentine Dance consisted of hundreds of yards of China silk which she let billow around her while lighting effects suggested that it was catching fire and taking shapes reminiscent of flowers, clouds, birds, and butterflies."
 Alas, other, lesser dancers soon appropriated her work for their own performances, prompting a frustrated Fuller to tour Europe in hopes of gaining more recognition for her art. Her performance at the  Folies Bergère in Paris was a smashing success, and she became a regular there, performing her "Fire Dance" as well as the "Serpentine Dance." She was a fixture of the local  Art Nouveau scene, hanging out with such luminaries as Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec, Auguste Rodin, Stéphane Mallarmé, and many others contemporary artists and writers. She was also a mentor and friend to American dancer  Isadora Duncan and weathered public criticism over her decision to live openly with her lesbian partner.
    Pierre and Marie Curie attended one of Fuller's performances at the Folies Bergère and were greatly impressed. The admiration was mutual: Fuller was so captivated by the Curies' radium experiments that she wrote to them in 1905, asking about the possibility of making a costume out of radium (she was unaware of just how limited a supply was in existence). Marie politely advised against it, although she herself liked to carry around vials of radium and enjoyed visiting the lab at night because "the glowing tubes looked like fairy lights." Undeterred, Fuller worked fluorescent salts into a black gauze dress that she wore to perform her " Radium Dance," creating the illusion of twinkling stars or ghostly lights surrounding her as she swirled on a darkened stage.
    At first glance, the two women don't seem to have much in common, but with  Radiant, Heinecke has teased out some intriguing parallels between them as they transformed their respective fields and forged an unlikely friendship. Ars sat down with Heinecke to learn more.
 Ars Technica: Marie Curie is practically a household name, but Loïe Fuller is not. So I'm curious what drove you to bring these two women together in a single book?
 Liz Heinecke: I was reading Eve Curie's biography of her mother and ran across Loïe Fuller's name. I was an art major in college. My daughter is a theater kid, and I love watching dance, although I'm not a dancer myself. I googled Loïe Fuller and was fascinated right away. Eventually, I found the New York Public Library of Performing Arts database and discovered they had a little bit of her writing available to look at online. And I discovered that she was interested in radioactivity, that she had met Thomas Edison, that she was a friend of Auguste Rodin. She knew all these interesting people, and I hadn't ever heard of her. She basically pioneered modern stage lighting. When I discovered that Marie and Loïe were born and died within five years of each other, I decided to write about the two of them together and have their stories intersect in parallel narratives.
 Ars Technica: Marie Curie and Loïe Fuller met in person at least once, but what do you see as the binding connection between them?
 Liz Heinecke: Curiosity. Loïe was an extremely curious person. Ultimately Loïe was probably the driving force in staying connected to Pierre and Marie because she really loved science. She wasn't well-educated, but she was very smart and knew that technology was the thing that made her stand out. She went to Marie and Pierre's labs because she was very curious about radium and radioactivity. I think Marie was curious about Loie's world too. We know that Marie and Pierre enjoyed art and theater and that Loïe took them to Rodin's studio to see his work.
 Both of them were far from perfect. Marie made mistakes in her life, but she was a more serious, careful person, and Loïe was just kind of out there. But I love that contrast, and I wrote them that way. I mean, if you read what Loïe wrote about Marie, she always felt she would never gossip about Marie. She always wrote, "Marie is very private. I want to respect her." She did not care as much what people thought about her, as long as people came to her shows.
  Ars Technica: You chose to write  Radiant very much in the style of a novel, even though it is technically nonfiction. Why is that?
    Liz Heinecke: I love a good nonfiction narrative, particularly about science. When I started thinking about the story, Loïe in particular, it's so visual. I imagined Loïe dancing on stage. I could picture Marie and Pierre in their lab surrounded by tubes of glowing radium. So I really wanted to write it so it read more like fiction. I felt like I would be able to reach a different audience, writing a book that read more like a novel rather than a straight science narrative or a straight biography.
 I state in the beginning of the book that the dialogue is invented, both internal and external. I wrote dialogue based on what I had learned about the two women. After a year reading every document I could find, I felt I almost knew them. I knew that Loïe drank black coffee and Marie drank tea, what they ate at different parts of their lives, and what they worried about. But I tried to base all the dialogue on the facts that I had uncovered. Certainly all of the scenes are based on factual events in their lives that actually happened. For instance, even if you read about Loïe Fuller, no one really talks about the fact that she danced on top of the Eiffel Tower during the summer solstice. There's so much science involved in the Eiffel Tower, I could have written a whole book just about that one night and all the interesting people that were there.
 Ars Technica: I did not know that Loïe Fuller had met with Thomas Edison. It makes sense because he was involved in cutting-edge sound and lighting as well as moving pictures, and she was interested the scientific aspects of her art. We have had this notion that there are  two cultures ever since C.P. Snow coined the term in 1959. But fundamentally, science is culture, and vice versa. Loïe Fuller kind of encapsulates that.
 Liz Heinecke: She does. She was so good at bringing different technologies together and creating something new. You could almost write a parallel story about Loïe and Edison. Neither of them was very well-educated, but they were both the predecessors of today's most popular people on social media. They were both extremely good at self-promotion. They both cultivated their images very carefully. They strike me as being very similar.
 I love when different disciplines come together. I feel like society today has become so compartmentalized, and in Paris in 1900, it was sort of that way, too. The scientists mostly hung out with the scientists. I thought it was so cool that Marie Curie opened herself and her world up to this dancer—someone completely outside of her sphere. It really enriched both of their lives. I feel like that's when you see huge explosions of creativity—when people move outside of their own spheres.
  Ars Technica: Has writing this book inspired you to try to recapture some of that Belle Époque interdisciplinary spirit?
 Liz Heinecke: There's nothing more fun than going to the library and reading through old documents, trying to find something new that no one has discovered yet. And there are so many scientific experiments you could try at home. I'm currently writing a physics books for kids, and there's one lab that's about creating thin layers on glass. And I thought, "Cool, let's try to put thin layers of gelatin on glass and color it and see if we can make slides that you could hold up [to the light]," like Loïe's multi-colored lighting.
 It's also easy to make an electroscope, the foundational concept of the equipment that the Curies used to measure radioactivity, which gives off an electrical charge. They used a version of an electroscope to spin a mirror that would let them measure how radioactive different materials were. That's what allowed Marie to make the first real measurements of radioactivity.
 I love how people from that era were able to see things very close-up with microscopes and very far away with telescopes for the first time. They could recognize that certain things under a microscope might look like a celestial object. Loïe would take things that she had seen in a scientist's lab or under a microscope and project it on a stage. Artists are still doing that. There's gorgeous art that modern scientists make with their microscopes and telescopes today.
title: 当物理学遇上舞蹈时：玛丽·居里（Marie Curie）和露易·富勒（LoïFuller）
title_en: When physics met dance: Marie Curie and Loïe Fuller in Belle Époque Paris

全球最大的投资者希望污染行业设定减排目标并实现净零排放
    贝莱德（BlackRock）是全球最大的投资者，曾表示，石油公司和其他污染行业应披露其碳排放量，并设定减排目标，这是资产管理公司迅速重新评估气候风险的最新迹象。
 这家投资公司在概述其计划的一封信中说，预计贝莱德投资的所有公司都将披露其运营和购买的能源的直接排放，分别称为范围1和范围2排放。
  贝莱德说，化石燃料提取器的目标应该是减少产品燃烧时释放的碳排放，这称为范围3排放。
 贝莱德是全球最具影响力的投资者之一，因为它拥有大量积极管理和与指数挂钩的基金。截至12月底，其控制的股票，债券和其他资产价值为8.7tn亿美元（合6.4tn英镑），包括持有的数十亿美元的石油公司。
 大多数主要的上市石油公司已经宣布了披露其碳排放量的计划，并按照世界上许多国家政府设定的到2050年实现净零碳的目标减少排放量。
 BP计划到2050年将其碳排放量几乎减少到零，这部分是通过碳捕获计划和自然恢复来抵消碳排放。它还承诺到2025年将低碳投资增长八倍，到2030年将十倍增长，同时在未来十年内将其化石燃料产量削减40％。 
法国石油公司道达尔已经对可再生能源进行了数十亿英镑的投资，并且拥有任何大型石油公司中最大的清洁能源项目管道之一。
 壳牌因提出模糊的气候计划而受到批评，该计划在短期而不是直接的排放数字上依赖“碳强度”目标。它计划到2050年实现绝对零净值。
 被视为气候落后者的美国石油公司埃克森（Exxon）致力于披露其排放量，并为减少排放量，甲烷和天然气燃烧制定了近期目标。与壳牌一样，目标是基于碳强度而不是绝对排放量。
 长期以来，贝莱德一直是激进主义者的批评焦点，这些激进主义者认为贝莱德没有履行职责。但是在去年，它突然开始以气候为由对公司投了反对票。 2020年，它投票反对了69家碳密集型公司的管理层，包括重新选举64位董事。
 贝莱德（BlackRock）上个月表示，其目标是到2050年在其投资组合中实现零碳净排放，并且最终将考虑撤出未采取行动的污染者。
 贝莱德表示，公司应制定严格的短期，中期和长期目标。它着重指出了联合国支持的基于科学的目标计划，这是经过审计和可靠的减排计划的典型例子。以科学为基础的目标旨在使公司与2015年《巴黎协定》的目标保持一致，以将全球温度上升限制在2C以下。
  激进主义者对贝莱德政策中的更多细节表示欢迎，但表示应坚持减少排放量，以确保污染者更快地行动。 
竞选组织ShareAction的金融部门战略主管Wolfgang Kuhn说：“这些要求是完全必要的，现在是时候将它们始终如一地纳入公司业务了。 问题在于，它们根本不足以使我们脱离困境。 目标必须雄心勃勃，公司需要说明如何减少排放。  “如果没有减少排放的具体步骤，那么净零排放的行动只不过是侧面的报告而已。”
note_en:
World’s biggest investor wants polluting industries to set targets to cut emissions and reach net zero
    BlackRock, the world’s biggest investor, has said that oil companies and other polluting industries should disclose their carbon emissions and set targets to cut them, in the latest sign of the rapid reassessment of climate risks by asset managers.
 All companies in which BlackRock invests will be expected to disclose direct emissions from operations and from energy they buy, known respectively as scope 1 and scope 2 emissions, the investment firm said in a letter outlining its plans.
  Fossil fuel extractors should base targets to cut emissions on the carbon released when their products are burned, known as scope 3 emissions, BlackRock said.
 BlackRock is one of the most influential investors in the world because of its vast actively managed and index-linked funds. The shares, bonds and other assets it controls were worth $8.7tn (£6.4tn) at the end of December, including  holdings of billions of dollars in oil companies.
 Most major listed oil companies have already announced plans to disclose their carbon emissions, and reduce them in line with the target to reach net zero carbon by 2050 set by many governments across the world.
 BP plans to cut its carbon emissions to virtually zero by 2050, in part by offsetting emissions through carbon capture schemes and natural restoration. It has also promised to grow low-carbon investments eightfold by 2025, and tenfold by 2030, while cutting its fossil fuel output by 40% in the next decade.
 French oil firm Total has made several multibillion pound investments in renewable energy, and has one of the largest pipelines of clean energy projects of any major oil company.
 Shell has been criticised for putting forward climate plans which are vague, and rely on “carbon intensity” targets in the near-term rather than outright emissions figures. It plans to reach net zero in absolute terms by 2050.
 US oil company Exxon, considered a climate laggard, has committed to disclosing its emissions and has set near-term targets for reducing emissions, methane and gas flaring. Like Shell, the targets are based on carbon intensity rather than absolute emissions.
 BlackRock has long been a  focus for criticism from activists who argued that it was not fulfilling its responsibilities. But in the last year it has started to vote against companies on climate grounds, in an abrupt turnaround. In 2020 it voted against management at 69 carbon-intensive companies, including on the re-election of 64 directors.
 BlackRock said last month it would target net zero carbon emissions in its portfolio by 2050, and that it would  eventually consider divestment from the polluters who did not take action.
 Companies should set rigorous short, medium and long-term targets, BlackRock said. It highlighted the  UN-backed science-based targets initiative as the leading example of an audited and reliable emissions reductions programme. Science-based targets are set to align companies with the aims of the 2015 Paris agreement of limiting global temperature rises to below 2C.
  Activists welcomed the extra detail in BlackRock’s policy, but said it should insist on more specific reductions to ensure polluters move more rapidly.
 Wolfgang Kuhn, director of financial sector strategies at ShareAction, a campaign group, said: “These asks are totally necessary, and it is high time they were included in company engagement on a consistent basis. The issue is that they are in no way sufficient to get us out of the hole; the targets need to be ambitious, and companies need to explain how they are going about reducing their emissions.
 “Without concrete steps to reduce emissions, action for net zero doesn’t amount to much more than a bit of reporting on the side.”
title: 贝莱德表示，石油公司应披露碳排放量
title_en: Oil firms should disclose carbon output, says BlackRock

TikTok被指控向儿童提供“隐藏”广告形式，这是BEUC的一系列正式投诉之一，BEUC是整个欧盟范围内的消费者监管机构。
 BEUC还指责该视频共享应用程序的隐私条款不明确，未能保护孩子免受不当内容的侵害，并以应用内货币剥夺了他们的生命……
   欧洲消费者组织（BEUC）总干事莫妮克·戈恩斯（Monique Goyens）在周二的一份声明中表示：“在短短几年内，TikTok已成为欧洲最受欢迎的社交媒体应用程序之一，拥有数百万用户。” “但是TikTok通过大规模侵犯他们的权利来让用户失望。我们发现了一系列侵犯消费者权益的行为，因此对TikTok提出了申诉。”
  藏匿广告的问题一直在这里发挥着作用，消费者倡导者将TikTok定位为其品牌主题标签挑战，目标受众是鼓舞者鼓励用户制作涉及特定产品的内容。
   BEUC瞄准了TikTok的虚拟礼物系统，用户可以通过该系统购买硬币，然后将其用于为创作者购买礼物，例如虚拟熊猫或“彩虹p”。
 消费者组织表示：“ TikTok声称……拥有改变硬币和礼物之间的汇率的绝对权利，有可能使金融交易对自己有利。”
 另一个投诉似乎没有说服力。 BEUC表示，TikTok的条款和条件允许其无偿分发用户内容，但这是社交媒体应用程序所针对的，并且是所有应用程序中的标准术语。 
TikTok一直在回应人们的担忧，但是最近加强了对青少年的隐私保护，其中最强有力的措施适用于13至15岁的青少年所拥有的帐户。  您正在阅读9to5Mac，这是专家，日复一日地发布有关Apple及其周围生态系统的新闻。 请务必查看我们的主页以获取所有最新新闻，并在Twitter，Facebook和LinkedIn上关注9to5Mac以保持循环。 不知道从哪里开始？ 查看我们的独家故事，评论，操作方法，并订阅我们的YouTube频道
note_en:
TikTok is accused of presenting kids with “hidden” forms of advertising as one of a number of formal complaints from the BEUC, an umbrella body for consumer watchdogs across the European Union.
 BEUC also accuses the video sharing app of unclear  privacy terms, failing to protect kids from inappropriate content, and ripping them off with in-app currency…
   “In just a few years, TikTok has become one of the most popular social media apps with millions of users across Europe,” said Monique Goyens, the director general of the European Consumer Organization (BEUC) said in a Tuesday statement. “But TikTok is letting its users down by breaching their rights on a massive scale. We have discovered a whole series of consumer rights infringements and therefore filed a complaint against TikTok.”
  The ever-present issue of hidden advertising is also in play here, with the consumer advocates targeting TikTok over its branded hashtag challenges, where influencers encourage users to make content involving specific products.
   BEUC is taking aim at TikTok’s virtual gift system, through which users can purchase coins that can then be used to buy gifts such as a virtual panda or “rainbow puke” for creators.
 “TikTok claims… an absolute right to modify the exchange rate between the coins and the gifts, potentially skewing the financial transaction in its own favor,” the consumer group said.
 Another complaint seems less persuasive. BEUC says that TikTok’s terms and conditions allow it to distribute user content without compensation, but that’s what social media apps are for, and is a standard term across all of them.
 TikTok has been responding to concerns, however, recently  boosting privacy protections for teenagers, with the strongest measures applying to accounts held by 13- to 15-year-olds.

 Epic写道：“向欧洲委员会竞争总局提起的申诉称，通过一系列精心设计的反竞争限制，苹果不仅损害了应用，而且完全消除了应用分发和支付流程中的竞争。” “苹果公司利用对iOS生态系统的控制权，在阻止竞争对手的同时使自己受益，其行为是滥用支配地位，违反了欧盟竞争法。”
 它不是在寻求对苹果的赔偿，而是希望欧盟竞争主管部门对其所称的iPhone制造商的“垄断渠道”采取补救措施。
  Epic Games创始人兼首席执行官蒂姆·斯威尼（Tim Sweeney）在一份声明中说：“这里面临的威胁是移动平台的未来。” “消费者有权从自己选择​​的来源安装应用程序，而开发人员有权在公平的市场中竞争。我们不会袖手旁观，并允许苹果利用其平台优势来控制什么应该是一个公平的数字竞争环境。这对那些由于商店之间缺乏竞争和应用内付款处理而完全缺乏竞争的消费者来说是不利的。对于开发人员来说，这是不利的，因为他们的生计往往取决于苹果公司在谁可以在iOS平台上以及在什么条件上获准的完全自由裁量权。”
 在苹果从App Store禁止Fortnite之后，Epic于去年8月对美国提起了诉讼。 
在Epic试图通过在Fortnite中添加其自己的支付机制以允许用户直接购买游戏内货币的方式绕过其应用程序内购买框架（并规避Apple的砍价）后，这家科技巨头采取了行动。这直接违反了Apple的规定。
 除了禁止Fortnight之外，苹果公司还表示，它将进一步取消Epic的开发人员帐户，并为其Unreal Engine使用开发人员工具，此举将影响依赖Epic引擎的第三方应用程序制造商。但是，它不能走那么远。
 一名美国法官迅速否认了Epic强迫苹果解除游戏禁令的动议，但库比蒂诺被命令不要阻止Epic在iOS上提供和分发其虚幻引擎的能力–限制了苹果采取焦土方法试图关闭Epic战斗的能力。
  从那以后，Epic在澳大利亚和英国对苹果提起法律诉讼。现在，它也在向欧盟监管机构请愿。
 与此同时，欧盟的反托拉斯部门于去年夏天对苹果公司进行了正式调查-一年多后，总部位于欧洲的音乐流媒体服务Spotify就“限制性” App Store规则提出类似的投诉，并且库比蒂诺（Cupertino）将iOS削减30％应用内付款。
 欧盟委员会当时表示，一位未具名的电子书/有声读物发行商也抱怨App Store规则对竞争的影响。
 它今天证实，它已收到Epic Games对苹果的投诉。欧盟委员会发言人告诉我们：“我们将根据我们的标准程序对其进行评估。” 
Epic的论点是，苹果拒绝在iOS上的Fortnight用户在Apple付款和Epic直接付款之间做出选择-声称储蓄将转移给直接购买者（尽管Epic当然可以赚钱，但前提是它可以绕开Apple削减开支的渠道）应用内付款）。
 Epic还试图推动苹果公司使其在iOS上运营Epic Games Store-苹果公司拒绝了此举，理由是该公司声称“确切的安全性，隐私权和内容标准”是基于App Store规则制定的（尽管Apple声称等同于“质量”的策展方案并不总是符合其允许在其App Store上运作的现实。
 早在2019年，苹果还推出了自己的游戏分发服务Apple Arcade，这是一种纯播放内容播放器，可访问可在Apple设备生态系统中播放的新游戏和独家游戏。
 这一举动或许是稻草，打破了骆驼相对于史诗游戏公司（Epic Games）的决定，后者决定全力以赴与苹果进行反托拉斯诉讼。 （其博客文章引用了Apple Arcade，并指出Apple禁止竞争对手（包括自己）这样做。）
 值得注意的是，Epic还与Google竞争，后者同样削减了通过其Play商店分发的Android应用的应用内付款-并于去年从Play商店中删除了Fortnight。
 然而，谷歌的Android平台允许第三方应用程序和替代应用程序商店的侧面加载，可以说使得反托拉斯案的难度加大，而苹果公司则施加了更严格的限制。
 不过，与此同时，Android主导了智能手机的市场份额，而苹果在全球市场的份额还不到五分之一。
note_en:
In a  blog post today the maker of the popular online game Fortnite said it’s extending its battle for what it dubbed “fairer digital platform practices for developers and consumers” to Europe, noting the bloc is already looking into competition concerns attached to the Apple App Store (and its payment service, Apple Pay).
   Regional lawmakers have also recently set out a  plan to expand platform regulation to put specific strictures on ‘gatekeeper’ platforms with the aim of ensuring fairness and accountability vis-a-vis third parties. And the issue of platform power is certainly one that’s now under close scrutiny by regulators and lawmakers around the world.
 We’re bringing our fight to end Apple’s App Store monopoly to Europe. Apple’s practices are harming consumers and app developers in Europe and around the world, and we’re joining the  #EU’s ongoing investigation into Apple’s abuse of its dominant position  https://t.co/LIb346QmEi
 “The complaint, filed with the European Commission’s Directorate-General for Competition, alleges that through a series of carefully designed anti-competitive restrictions, Apple has not just harmed but completely eliminated competition in app distribution and payment processes,” Epic writes, adding: “Apple uses its control of the iOS ecosystem to benefit itself while blocking competitors and its conduct is an abuse of a dominant position and in breach of EU competition law.”
 It’s not seeking damages against Apple but wants EU competition authorities to impose remedies against what it describes as the iPhone maker’s “monopoly channels”.
  “What’s at stake here is the very future of mobile platforms,” said Epic Games founder and CEO, Tim Sweeney, in a statement. “Consumers have the right to install apps from sources of their choosing and developers have the right to compete in a fair marketplace. We will not stand idly by and allow Apple to use its platform dominance to control what should be a level digital playing field. It’s bad for consumers, who are paying inflated prices due to the complete lack of competition among stores and in-app payment processing. And it’s bad for developers, whose very livelihoods often hinge on Apple’s complete discretion as to who to allow on the iOS platform, and on which terms.”
 Epic launched a US lawsuit against Apple  last August after Apple banned Fortnite from the App Store.
 The tech giant made the move after Epic tried to bypass its in-app purchase framework (and circumvent the cut Apple takes) by adding its own payment mechanism to Fortnite to let users purchase in-game currency directly — in direct contravention of Apple’s rules.
 As well as banning Fortnight, Apple said it would go further and revoke Epic’s developer account and access to developer tools for its Unreal Engine — a move that would have affected third party app makers that rely on Epic’s engine. However it was barred from going that far.
 A US judge quickly  denied Epic’s motion to force Apple to unblock the game but Cupertino was ordered not to block Epic’s ability to provide and distribute its Unreal Engine on iOS — limiting Apple’s ability to take a scorched earth approach to try to shut Epic’s battle down.
  Since then Epic has filed legal complaints against Apple in Australia and the UK. It’s now also petitioning EU regulators.
 The EU’s antitrust division, meanwhile, opened a formal investigation of Apple last summer — more than a year after the Europe-based music streaming service  Spotify had made a similar complaint over ‘restrictive’ App Store rules and the 30% cut Cupertino takes on iOS in-app payments.
 The Commission said at the time that an unnamed e-book/audiobook distributor had also complained about the impact of App Store rules on competition.
 It confirmed today that it has received a complaint  by Epic Games against Apple.     “  We will assess it based on our standard procedures,” a Commission spokesperson told us.
 Epic’s argument is that Apple is denying Fortnight users on iOS a choice between Apple payment and Epic direct payment — claiming savings would be passed to direct purchasers (although Epic of course stands to gain money if it can open up a channel that bypasses Apple’s cut on in-app payments).
 Epic has also tried to  push Apple to let it operate an Epic Games Store on iOS — a move Apple refused, citing the “exacting standards for security, privacy, and content” which it argues are predicated on the App Store rules (although Apple’s claims of curation equaling ‘quality’ don’t always live up to  the reality of what it allows to operate on its App Store).
 Back in  2019, Apple also launched its own gaming distribution service, Apple Arcade — a pure-play content play that offers access to new and exclusive games playable across Apple’s device ecosystem.
 That move was perhaps the straw the broke the camel’s back vis-a-vis Epic Games deciding to go all in on an antitrust brawl with Apple. (Its blog post references Apple Arcade, and notes that Apple has barred competitors, including itself, from doing the same).
 It’s worth noting that Epic has also squared up to Google, which similarly takes a cut of in-app payments of Android apps distributed via its Play Store — and which also removed Fortnight from the Play Store  last year.
 However Google’s Android platform allows sideloading of third party apps and alternative app stores, arguably making it harder to make an antitrust case stick vs the tighter restrictions applied by Apple.
 At the same time, though, Android dominates smartphone marketshare — while Apple’s cut of the global market is less than a fifth.
title: Epic Games将其Apple App Store之战带到了欧洲
title_en: Epic Games takes its Apple App Store fight to Europe

note:
新数据显示，2020年是Chromebook销量超过Macs的第一年，市场份额大幅增长，而Windows却遭受了损失。由Google的Chrome操作系统驱动的计算机在各个季度之前的销售量已经超过了苹果的计算机，但是2020年是Chrome操作系统排在第二位的第一年。微软的Windows仍然占据了多数市场份额，但由于Chrome操作系统和macOS都获得了份额，因此受到了很大的冲击。
 里程碑基于IDC提供的数字，通常不会根据设备操作系统来划分销售量。但是，当我们去寻找这种流行病可能如何影响PC市场时，IDC分析师Mike Shirer向GeekWire证实了这一发现。 （我们还与Gartner联系，但该公司并未将Chromebook纳入其传统的PC市场。）
 这对谷歌来说是一个巨大的胜利，对苹果和微软都是一个警告。它还向应用程序和游戏开发人员发出信号，表明Chrome操作系统不再被忽略。坦白说，任何通过互联网提供产品或服务的公司都应预留资源，以确保Chrome操作系统的体验可与Windows和macOS相提并论。
    2020年第一季度，苹果和谷歌并驾齐驱：Windows占据了87.5％的市场份额，macOS占据了5.8％的市场份额，Chrome OS占据了5.3％的市场份额。但是在2020年第二季度，Windows下降至81.7％，macOS增长至7.6％，Chrome OS跃升至10.0％。
 2020年第三季度和2020年第四季度证实了这一趋势：Windows在第三季度进一步下降至78.9％，然后在第四季度下降至76.7％； macOS在第三季度增长到8.4％，然后在第四季度回落到7.7％，而Chrome OS在第三季度占11.5％，然后在第四季度占14.4％。第四季度的业绩尤为突出，因为第四季度往往是PC出货量最大的季度。尽管macOS在2019年第四季度扩大了其在Chrome操作系统方面的领先优势，但Chrome操作系统在2020年第四季度卷土重来。
    高于MacOS的Chrome OS连续四分之三成为趋势。可以肯定地说，来自宏cer，华硕，戴尔，惠普和联想等PC制造商的Chromebook现在已经超过了苹果的台式机Mac和笔记本电脑MacBook。也就是说，2020年不是典型的一年。
  在经历了数年的坏消息之后，PC出货量在2019年出现增长，只是在2020年第一季度冠状病毒大流行削减了庆祝活动。由于中国（全球最大的PC供应商）COVID-19的爆发，PC的供应量大增。受到打击，导致数年来最大幅度的下降。然后在2020年第二季度和当年剩余时间里，解决了供应限制，家庭和远程学习工作的增加推动了企业和消费者对PC的需求。 
大流行是技术趋势的加速。 Chrome操作系统在过去几年中一直在增长，因此，它在2020年甚至会更快地增长并不令人感到震惊。问题是：这种增长是否会持续下去，并且会以Windows的费用为代价吗？尽管PC的出货量全面增加，但它表明macOS也在获得份额，而Windows则几十年来首次跌破80％的门槛（过去两个季度，尽管该年度仍高于80％） 。
 Chrome操作系统挑战Windows在低成本笔记本电脑市场中的主导地位，这是传统PC销量出现较大反弹的部分原因，也是Microsoft将Windows 10X最初在单屏设备上首次亮相的部分原因。
  在大流行之前，Chrome操作系统的成功主要限于美国学校。 2020年的需求似乎已经扩大到美国PC市场这一很小但至关重要的部分。在接下来的几个月中，我们将了解Chrome OS的收益是否仅限于美国学校，整个美国，世界各地的学校，或者Google的低成本笔记本电脑是否正在遍及全球。期望苹果和微软相应地调整各自的策略。
note_en:
New numbers show 2020 was the first year that Chromebooks outsold Macs, posting impressive market share gains at the expense of Windows. Computers powered by Google’s Chrome OS have outsold Apple’s computers in individual quarters before, but 2020 was the first full year that Chrome OS took second place. Microsoft’s Windows still retained majority market share, but also took a big hit as both Chrome OS and macOS gained share.
 The milestone is based on numbers provided by IDC, which doesn’t typically break out sales based on device operating system. But when we went looking to see how the pandemic may have impacted the PC market, IDC analyst Mike Shirer confirmed the findings to GeekWire. (We also contacted Gartner but that firm does not include Chromebooks in its traditional PC market results.)
 This is a big win for Google and a warning for both Apple and Microsoft. It also signals to app and game developers that Chrome OS can no longer be ignored. Frankly, any business that provides a product or service over the internet should be setting aside resources to ensure the Chrome OS experience is comparable to Windows and macOS.
    In Q1 2020, Apple and Google were neck-and-neck: Windows grabbed 87.5% market share, macOS took 5.8%, and Chrome OS captured 5.3%. But in Q2 2020, Windows fell to 81.7%, macOS grew to 7.6%, and Chrome OS jumped to 10.0%.
 Q3 2020 and Q4 2020 confirmed the trend: Windows dropped further to 78.9% for Q3 and then 76.7% for Q4; macOS grew to 8.4% for Q3 and then fell back to 7.7% for Q4, while Chrome OS had 11.5% for Q3 and then 14.4% share for Q4. The Q4 results are particularly notable as the fourth quarter tends to be the biggest for PC shipments. While macOS widened its lead over Chrome OS in Q4 2019, Chrome OS came roaring back in Q4 2020.
    Three quarters in a row of Chrome OS above macOS makes a trend. It’s safe to say that Chromebooks from PC makers like Acer, Asus, Dell, HP, and Lenovo now outsell Apple’s range of desktop Macs and laptop MacBooks. That said, 2020 was not a typical year.
  After years of bad news, PC shipments saw  growth in 2019, only to have the coronavirus pandemic cut the celebration short in  Q1 2020. Due to the outbreak of COVID-19 in China (the world’s largest supplier of PCs), PC supply took a hit, leading to the sharpest decline in years. Then in  Q2 2020 and the rest of the year, supply constraints were addressed and the rise in work from home and distance learning drove PC demand from businesses and consumers alike.
 The pandemic is an accelerant of tech trends. Chrome OS has been growing over the past few years so it’s not shocking that it is growing even faster in 2020. The question is: Will that growth continue, and will it come at Windows’ expense? While PC shipments are up across the board, it’s telling that macOS has also been gaining share while Windows fell below the 80% threshold for the first time in decades (in the past two quarters, though it was still above 80% for the year).
 Chrome OS challenging Windows’ dominance of the low-cost laptop market is part of the bigger rebound of traditional PC sales, and part of the reason why Microsoft shifted  Windows 10X to debut initially on single-screen devices.
  Until the pandemic, Chrome OS’ success was largely limited to U.S. schools. Demand in 2020 appears to have expanded beyond that small but critical part of the U.S. PC market. Over the coming months, we’ll find out whether Chrome OS’ gains are limited to U.S. schools, the U.S. at large, schools worldwide, or whether Google’s low-cost laptops are making inroads everywhere. Expect Apple and Microsoft to adjust their respective strategies accordingly.
title: IDC：Chrome操作系统的年度市场份额在2020年首次超过macOS，同比增长4.4％至10.8％，Windows同比下降4.9％至80.5％，macOS同比增长0.8％至7.5％
title_en: IDC: Chrome OS annual market share passed macOS for the first time in 2020, up 4.4% YoY to 10.8%, Windows fell 4.9% YoY to 80.5%, macOS up 0.8% YoY to 7.5%
